## [00:00 - 03:01]

Welcome to the wonderful world of course creation. I am so excited to walk you through the creation of your course and I'm so glad you're here. I'm also really honored that Dean has invited me to cover this topic for you. I'm Lane Amon, director of content at mastermind.com and over the course of my career I've created hundreds of different courses of different lengths and at different price points on different topics, everything from scrapbooking to email marketing and I'm thrilled to be here with you today. You can be making sales 24 hours a day, 365 days a year on automatic pilot. Next, courses can grow with you. You can create a simple course now and then you can add to it or add additional courses or additional lessons to your original course over time as your expertise grows or as your audience asks for additional guidance. Courses can fit into your marketing funnel at any price point. You can offer them for free to use as a lead generator or they can be a high-end offer or you can offer them as a bonus for other purposes. For instance, you can offer a course as a bonus for those who sign up for your coaching program or your mastermind. In sum, I believe everyone should have a course as part of their business repertoire. It's a lot of ground to cover and it's going to be great. But one of the first things we need to cover is, what is a course anyway? At its most basic, an online course is your content organized and compiled in a format where it can be delivered and consumed through the internet. There are different flavors of courses. There are on-demand courses where people go through the material at their own speed and on their own schedule like this one. There are instructor-led courses where all students start and stop at the same time, like an online course through a college. There are live courses where the material is delivered in real time. There are pre-recorded courses where the material is prepared and recorded ahead of time and posted for people to access, again like mastermind business system, and there are all types in between.

We think we're not good enough or we're faking it. But you know what? Everyone feels this way at some point. Even the most talented, the most experienced, and the most knowledgeable people sometimes feel like they don't know what the heck they're doing. In fact, there's something called the Dunning-Kruger Effect that basically says the more you know, the less confident you are in your knowledge because you know how much else there is for you to learn. And the less you know, the more confident you are. So in other words, if you're feeling a little unsure, that's actually a sign that you do know your stuff. So don't let the lack of certainty keep you from moving forward. Sure, maybe in an ideal world, you would know everything before you create your course. But first of all, you can't ever know everything. And second, you can't help anyone if you don't get something out into the world. Think about teaching math. Just because a kindergarten teacher doesn't know linear algebra doesn't mean they can't do a great job teaching addition and subtraction, which have to be mastered before people can move on to linear algebra.
